metadata.title: Multilingual public diplomacy: Strategic communication of Israeli Defence Forces (IDF) in Twitter during Operation Guardian of the Walls

abstract: Operation Guardian of the Walls was the most serious military conflict between the Israeli Defence Forces (IDF) and Palestinian armed groups since 2014. This article aims to explore the Organized Persuasive Communication (OPC) made by IDF, in English, Spanish and French, during the 11 days of the escalation of the war. For this purpose, it has resorted to techniques typical of computational science, specifically the unsupervised machine learning Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and sentiment analysis (multilingual). The data show that there are no significant differences between a range of official Twitter accounts giving a process of information uniformity. The results of the study allow us to know the scope of IDF's communication within the framework of the so-called new Israeli diplomacy. In addition, this text attempts to demonstrate the usefulness of text mining and Natural Language Processing (NLP) to strategic studies and international relations.

paragraphs: The judicial eviction of several Palestinian families from the Sheikh Jarrah neighbourhood of East Jerusalem started a new spiral of violence which, once again, put the media spotlight on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict in May 2021. The armed confrontation between Hamas operational forces (including Palestinian Islamic Jihad) and the Israel Defence Forces (IDF) resulted in 12 Israelis and 248 Palestinians being killed, including 67 minors and thousands of injured (Staff, 2021), the most severe armed conflict since Operation Protective Edge in 2014.

The IDF military operation, called Operation Guardian of the Walls, was continuously monitored by the Israeli army's Twitter account (@IDF, @FDIonline (Fuerzas de Defensa de Israel) and @Tsahal_IDF). In this sense, the scientific literature on the subject indicates that the Israeli army has made excellent profits from the exploitation of social networks. Heemsbergen and Lindgreen (2014) point out that the IDF, through the transparency of its missions, seeks to amplify the state's point of view to audiences potentially sympathetic to the Israeli position. Likewise, Jędrzejewska (2020) asserts that public diplomacy and external communication, called hasbara, is an external priority of Israel since it does not rely solely on military deterrence. However, regardless of the case study, the main objective of good institutional public communication within the framework of security is: 'to ensure that the information of the institution is transformed as little as possible in its passage through the media' (García-Marín, 2017: 57).

This article aims to study public diplomacy and the actions carried out by the state of Israel by studying the Twitter messages of the accounts managed by the Israeli army (in English, Spanish and French) during Operation Guardian of the Walls (N = 595). The aim is to make a theoretically novel contribution and demonstrate the usefulness of text mining tools to strategic studies and international relations. We will use a mixed methodological strategy combining the classic content analysis with different algorithmic models (such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation [LDA] and multilingual sentiment). In essence, there is a need to acknowledge the application of empirical and replicable analyses, aimed to verify and refute theories in the field of international relations.

The article is structured as follows. Following this introduction, there will be a theoretical review of public diplomacy and institutional security and defence communication, and its application within the Israeli case. Subsequently, the main methodological tools used for the analysis will be presented. Finally, the last two sections are dedicated to drawing the main conclusions of the research.

sections.paper_id: Moreno-Mercado and Calatrava-García - 2023 - Multilingual public diplomacy Strategic communication of Israeli Defence Forces (IDF) in Twitter du
paragraphs: Public diplomacy is one of the essential elements in the studies of politics and international relations today, not only from constructivist approaches, but also from more realistic and rationalist approaches (Tago, 2017). There is no consensus on this concept in the specialized literature, although a good definition would be from Sharp (2005: 106): 'the process by which direct relations with people in a country are pursued to advance the interests and extend the values of those being represented'. This definition encompasses the classic elements of public diplomacy: the government's communication with the foreign public, transmitting information and ideas, and values (Snow, 2009).

As in many social science concepts, there is a debate in the literature about its conceptual limits. Furthermore, this concept is a meeting point between several disciplines: international relations, political communication, strategic studies, or international information (Gilboa, 2008). What is undeniable is that there has been an evolution in consideration of this concept that allows us to speak of old public diplomacy and new public diplomacy. However, the limits are not very clear. Old public diplomacy was very focused on the propaganda campaigns of the Cold War, and the latter is closer to a more complex international world, with a more horizontal communication structure as people connect through networks established by new technologies. Although there is still a strategic horizon in state behaviour in today's world, information flows are becoming more complex as access to information has become paramount for all international actors. The new public diplomacy includes states and different sub-state actors and non-state groups, from NGOs to terrorist groups, all of which try to project their messages to achieve political objectives.

According to Gilboa, the elements of public diplomacy include advocacy, broadcasting, public relations, cultural diplomacy, exchange and national branding. In this article, we will study news and information management as a tool of advocacy and broadcasting. So, the IDF conducts public diplomacy through information management. This action carries out the promotion of national interests accomplishing several tasks and objectives, such as: (i) communicating the legitimacy of a course of action; (ii) changing the mind and behaviour of enemies; (iii) opposing the propaganda of the enemy; (iv) causing an enemy to surrender; (v) resolving a conflict and preventing a war in the first place; and (vi) developing international support, among others (Pratkanis, 2009: 114).

Whether new or old, public diplomacy should be included as one of the instruments of soft power, as an apolitical expression of this concept introduced in the 1990s, in the aftermath of the Cold War (Nye, 2004). Another way of defining this soft power would be that of power over opinion, which is increasingly important in this age of global information. In this sense, credibility is presented as an essential value for the effectiveness of this power (Melissen, 2005). This statement opens the door to many other theoretical and practical considerations which include objective and subjective components, such as clarity, accuracy and trustworthiness.

Although there is a separation between public diplomacy and propaganda in the specialized literature -propaganda should be understood in the field of deception and manipulation -on many occasions, both concepts overlap. Therefore, we can include these actions of the Israeli government within the so-called Organized Persuasive Communication (OPC), understood as all activities of organized persuasion, from advertising to strategic communication, information campaigns, or psychological operations (Bakir et al., 2018). This OPC is fundamental to power in all social spheres, including the international one.

So, OPC is a broad concept that includes non-manipulative (consensual) and manipulative (propaganda) forms of persuasion. The main manipulative forms would be deception+, incentivization and coercion. The border between the two forms is not as straightforward as it could be, though, because the very act of providing the information is a way to place the frame and the narrative we are seeking. In this sense, the tweets of the Israeli Armed Forces have at least a productive component, i.e. the messages are established as a nexus between knowledge and power, striving to impart a particular vision of reality. These messages, in this way and a facultative sense, produce and consolidate power asymmetries.

Institutional public communication from Israel could be subject to the same ideological filter as the conflict itself. It has been claimed that external communication aims to criminalize human rights defenders on the charge of anti-Semitism (Basallote-Marín, 2019: 194). Regardless of the feelings that may arise from the conflicting dynamics in Israel and Palestine, the truth is that Israeli institutional security and defence communication is a subject of broad academic debate. So-called 'Israeli strategic communication', which is significantly related to the concept of OPC, is traditionally based on the hasbara (public diplomacy) institution. However, in the last years, these institutions have had some failures achieving the objectives, so two new concepts have been included: medini'ut tziburit (new public diplomacy institutions and protocols) and hama'araha al hatoda'a (cognitive campaign) (Siman-Tov and Fridman, 2020: 24).

The hasbara concept is probably the most widely used and well-known term outside of Israel. There is unanimity that Nahum Solokow introduced the term (Cummings, 2016), although there have been numerous efforts to define and limit it theoretically. In this regard, Sabel (2017), in his review of Cummings' book Israel's Public Diplomacy: The Problems of Hasbara, 1966-1975, points out that the hasbara has been organically disorganized. Part of this concept's fame is because it is unique and aims to gain outside support through what Kuperwaiser and Siman-Tov (2019) call historical justice. In other words, we could extrapolate to the Israeli case what Colom-Piella (2019: 13) said about the Russian and American concepts of information warfare: 'The different strategic cultures and historical traditions that countries possess have conditioned the development of both approaches to information warfare.'

Historically, the hasbara has had successes and failures. On the one hand, it served to justify to international public opinion the pre-emptive attack on Egypt in the Six-Day War in 1967. But, on the other hand, it was not able to legitimize the Israeli stance during the Second Intifada (2000)(2001)(2002)(2003)(2004)(2005) or diplomatically capitalize on the military success in the 2006 Lebanon war (Vatikai, 2014). Today the effectiveness of hasbara is more discussed than ever. For, although the substance of the message is the same, there is a need to individualize the messages to potential audiences (Arabs, Americans, or Europeans) as Shoval (2007: 17) put it: 'Political or military hasbara is, after all, not Coca-Cola.' Firstly, because various military circles believe that the mere clarification of military offensives is not adapted to international criticism or the immediacy of the internet and the new media (Ben-Yishai, 2019). Furthermore, because the IDF has filled the institutional vacuum of Israeli public policy, preventing the development of effective public diplomacy, which provides a more inclusive view of foreign policy (Hadari and Turgeman, 2018).

During the 20th century, the hasbara also coexisted with the concept of cognitive campaigning. It is likely that the cognitive campaigns, framed within the classic information war, are the least original from a theoretical point of view. Israeli defence policy is based on the deterrence of aggression, with cyberspace and the information environment being one more pillar to be managed and controlled (Segell, 2021). A very illustrative example is the rally round the flag effect, which produces coverage of the hypothetical soft (non-military) BDS threats 1 within the Israeli media complex (Hatuel-Radoshitzky and Yarchi, 2020).

The use of psychological warfare, framed within the cognitive campaigns, has also left optimal benefits within the communicative machinery of the Hebrew country. Again, if we look at history, we see the idea that a hostile act has a forceful military response which works as a deterrent. In fact, according to Bar (2020: 340-341), Israeli military circles do not focus on debunking conspiracy myths and theories because they help deterrence itself. A famous example of success is the rumour spread by the IDF during the 1948 war that Israel had acquired an atomic weapon on the basis that some physicists in the Manhattan Project were Jewish and Albert Einstein was a pro-Zionist. However, as we pointed out before, adverse effects are also noteworthy in this information age. Similarly, the attacks in Gaza during the various military operations have given rise to certain theories which suggest that the Israelis practise State terrorism against civilians indiscriminately. These theories attempt to delegitimize Israeli theses that military operations are performed surgically against terrorist commandos. Perhaps the most famous of all is the so-called Dahiya Doctrine based on the destruction of civilian infrastructure with the aim of terrorizing the civilian population (Harrold, 2020) and destroying the nerve-line support of adversary armed groups. 2 Finally, the significant step forward of the Israeli communicative environment is known as the new public diplomacy, replacing the hasbara introduced in the mid-2000s (Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the State of Israel, 2008). The main difference that introduced this new concept for the IDF was to adapt to the digitized audiences that in many cases act as media. These changes were reflected in the presence of various digital platforms in different languages. However, the transition from hasbara to a new public diplomacy can be summarized in the following guidelines: (1) to focus on solving, in light of the international public opinion, establishing a multilevel dialogue with the audiences of the target country; (2) to praise the achievements of Israel in the political, military, cultural, academic and economic fields; (3) to integrate and mobilize civil organizations; (4) to consider the public opinion as an actor in the process of developing public policies; and (5) to integrate a formal monologue with informal dialogue (Siman-Tov and Fridman, 2020: 37-38).

As for the effectiveness of this public diplomacy, we also find contradictory, and often even conflicting, explanations. Rodríguez (2015) states that the virality of IDF social media messages is low (higher on Facebook than on Twitter). In addition, he points out that the IDF's accounts aim to highlight military achievements more than to provide information to citizens. Aouragh (2016: 294) states:

The underlying truth of colonialism, obscured by an ideological bias (Zionism), does not allow hasbara [public diplomacy or hasbara 2.0] to arrive at the most logical explanation that would be in tune with most public relations approaches or media analyses.

On the contrary, the transparency of the IDF's statistics serves as a legitimizing element in regard to the traditional media, as in various British and South African newspapers during the 2014 war in Gaza (Tasseron and Lawson, 2020).

The three terms used are fed back and interconnected if we analyse the public communication of the IDF at different historical moments. The 'success' and 'failure' of Israeli institutional communication depends, to a large extent, on the degree of polarization of the recipients in the face of the conflict itself. To cite an example, the account of the IDF spokesperson on Facebook, Avichai Adraee in Arabic, has served to humanize the enemy's face. Nevertheless, the interaction with the account is independent of the content it generates (Samuel-Azran and Yarchi, 2018). However, we must not forget that greater deliberation on political issues is related to greater polarization (Serrano-Contreras et al., 2020).

As can be inferred from the theoretical review, the institutional communication of the IDF requires up-to-date analyses which enrich the theoretical debate that persists to the present day. This scientific need is doubly essential. First, the unique situation of Israel, which has resulted in a very peculiar military culture (Jordán, 2015) oriented to pragmatism (Kover, 2011: 720-725). Moreover, information trends about armed conflicts tend to pay more attention to violent and dramatic stories than to their political contexts (Yarchi et al., 2013). For all these reasons, we consider this research novel within the area of communication and conflicts.

In this article, we will study news and information management as a tool of advocacy and broadcasting. So, the IDF conducts public diplomacy through information management. This action carries out the promotion of national interests accomplishing several tasks and objectives, such as: (i) communicating the legitimacy of a course of action; (ii) changing the mind and behaviour of enemies; (iii) opposing the propaganda of the enemy; (iv) causing an enemy to surrender; (v) resolving a conflict and preventing a war in the first place; and (vi) developing international support, among others (Pratkanis, 2009: 114).

Whether new or old, public diplomacy should be included as one of the instruments of soft power, as an apolitical expression of this concept introduced in the 1990s, in the aftermath of the Cold War (Nye, 2004). Another way of defining this soft power would be that of power over opinion, which is increasingly important in this age of global information. In this sense, credibility is presented as an essential value for the effectiveness of this power (Melissen, 2005). This statement opens the door to many other theoretical and practical considerations which include objective and subjective components, such as clarity, accuracy and trustworthiness.

Although there is a separation between public diplomacy and propaganda in the specialized literature -propaganda should be understood in the field of deception and manipulation -on many occasions, both concepts overlap. Therefore, we can include these actions of the Israeli government within the so-called Organized Persuasive Communication (OPC), understood as all activities of organized persuasion, from advertising to strategic communication, information campaigns, or psychological operations (Bakir et al., 2018). This OPC is fundamental to power in all social spheres, including the international one.

So, OPC is a broad concept that includes non-manipulative (consensual) and manipulative (propaganda) forms of persuasion. The main manipulative forms would be deception+, incentivization and coercion. The border between the two forms is not as straightforward as it could be, though, because the very act of providing the information is a way to place the frame and the narrative we are seeking. In this sense, the tweets of the Israeli Armed Forces have at least a productive component, i.e. the messages are established as a nexus between knowledge and power, striving to impart a particular vision of reality. These messages, in this way and a facultative sense, produce and consolidate power asymmetries.

Institutional public communication from Israel could be subject to the same ideological filter as the conflict itself. It has been claimed that external communication aims to criminalize human rights defenders on the charge of anti-Semitism (Basallote-Marín, 2019: 194). Regardless of the feelings that may arise from the conflicting dynamics in Israel and Palestine, the truth is that Israeli institutional security and defence communication is a subject of broad academic debate. So-called 'Israeli strategic communication', which is significantly related to the concept of OPC, is traditionally based on the hasbara (public diplomacy) institution. However, in the last years, these institutions have had some failures achieving the objectives, so two new concepts have been included: medini'ut tziburit (new public diplomacy institutions and protocols) and hama'araha al hatoda'a (cognitive campaign) (Siman-Tov and Fridman, 2020: 24).

The hasbara concept is probably the most widely used and well-known term outside of Israel. There is unanimity that Nahum Solokow introduced the term (Cummings, 2016), although there have been numerous efforts to define and limit it theoretically. In this regard, Sabel (2017), in his review of Cummings' book Israel's Public Diplomacy: The Problems of Hasbara, 1966-1975, points out that the hasbara has been organically disorganized. Part of this concept's fame is because it is unique and aims to gain outside support through what Kuperwaiser and Siman-Tov (2019) call historical justice. In other words, we could extrapolate to the Israeli case what Colom-Piella (2019: 13) said about the Russian and American concepts of information warfare: 'The different strategic cultures and historical traditions that countries possess have conditioned the development of both approaches to information warfare.'

Historically, the hasbara has had successes and failures. On the one hand, it served to justify to international public opinion the pre-emptive attack on Egypt in the Six-Day War in 1967. But, on the other hand, it was not able to legitimize the Israeli stance during the Second Intifada (2000)(2001)(2002)(2003)(2004)(2005) or diplomatically capitalize on the military success in the 2006 Lebanon war (Vatikai, 2014). Today the effectiveness of hasbara is more discussed than ever. For, although the substance of the message is the same, there is a need to individualize the messages to potential audiences (Arabs, Americans, or Europeans) as Shoval (2007: 17) put it: 'Political or military hasbara is, after all, not Coca-Cola.' Firstly, because various military circles believe that the mere clarification of military offensives is not adapted to international criticism or the immediacy of the internet and the new media (Ben-Yishai, 2019). Furthermore, because the IDF has filled the institutional vacuum of Israeli public policy, preventing the development of effective public diplomacy, which provides a more inclusive view of foreign policy (Hadari and Turgeman, 2018).

During the 20th century, the hasbara also coexisted with the concept of cognitive campaigning. It is likely that the cognitive campaigns, framed within the classic information war, are the least original from a theoretical point of view. Israeli defence policy is based on the deterrence of aggression, with cyberspace and the information environment being one more pillar to be managed and controlled (Segell, 2021). A very illustrative example is the rally round the flag effect, which produces coverage of the hypothetical soft (non-military) BDS threats 1 within the Israeli media complex (Hatuel-Radoshitzky and Yarchi, 2020).

The use of psychological warfare, framed within the cognitive campaigns, has also left optimal benefits within the communicative machinery of the Hebrew country. Again, if we look at history, we see the idea that a hostile act has a forceful military response which works as a deterrent. In fact, according to Bar (2020: 340-341), Israeli military circles do not focus on debunking conspiracy myths and theories because they help deterrence itself. A famous example of success is the rumour spread by the IDF during the 1948 war that Israel had acquired an atomic weapon on the basis that some physicists in the Manhattan Project were Jewish and Albert Einstein was a pro-Zionist. However, as we pointed out before, adverse effects are also noteworthy in this information age. Similarly, the attacks in Gaza during the various military operations have given rise to certain theories which suggest that the Israelis practise State terrorism against civilians indiscriminately. These theories attempt to delegitimize Israeli theses that military operations are performed surgically against terrorist commandos. Perhaps the most famous of all is the so-called Dahiya Doctrine based on the destruction of civilian infrastructure with the aim of terrorizing the civilian population (Harrold, 2020) and destroying the nerve-line support of adversary armed groups. 2 Finally, the significant step forward of the Israeli communicative environment is known as the new public diplomacy, replacing the hasbara introduced in the mid-2000s (Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the State of Israel, 2008). The main difference that introduced this new concept for the IDF was to adapt to the digitized audiences that in many cases act as media. These changes were reflected in the presence of various digital platforms in different languages. However, the transition from hasbara to a new public diplomacy can be summarized in the following guidelines: (1) to focus on solving, in light of the international public opinion, establishing a multilevel dialogue with the audiences of the target country; (2) to praise the achievements of Israel in the political, military, cultural, academic and economic fields; (3) to integrate and mobilize civil organizations; (4) to consider the public opinion as an actor in the process of developing public policies; and (5) to integrate a formal monologue with informal dialogue (Siman-Tov and Fridman, 2020: 37-38).

As for the effectiveness of this public diplomacy, we also find contradictory, and often even conflicting, explanations. Rodríguez (2015) states that the virality of IDF social media messages is low (higher on Facebook than on Twitter). In addition, he points out that the IDF's accounts aim to highlight military achievements more than to provide information to citizens. Aouragh (2016: 294) states:

The underlying truth of colonialism, obscured by an ideological bias (Zionism), does not allow hasbara [public diplomacy or hasbara 2.0] to arrive at the most logical explanation that would be in tune with most public relations approaches or media analyses.

On the contrary, the transparency of the IDF's statistics serves as a legitimizing element in regard to the traditional media, as in various British and South African newspapers during the 2014 war in Gaza (Tasseron and Lawson, 2020).

The three terms used are fed back and interconnected if we analyse the public communication of the IDF at different historical moments. The 'success' and 'failure' of Israeli institutional communication depends, to a large extent, on the degree of polarization of the recipients in the face of the conflict itself. To cite an example, the account of the IDF spokesperson on Facebook, Avichai Adraee in Arabic, has served to humanize the enemy's face. Nevertheless, the interaction with the account is independent of the content it generates (Samuel-Azran and Yarchi, 2018). However, we must not forget that greater deliberation on political issues is related to greater polarization (Serrano-Contreras et al., 2020).

As can be inferred from the theoretical review, the institutional communication of the IDF requires up-to-date analyses which enrich the theoretical debate that persists to the present day. This scientific need is doubly essential. First, the unique situation of Israel, which has resulted in a very peculiar military culture (Jordán, 2015) oriented to pragmatism (Kover, 2011: 720-725). Moreover, information trends about armed conflicts tend to pay more attention to violent and dramatic stories than to their political contexts (Yarchi et al., 2013). For all these reasons, we consider this research novel within the area of communication and conflicts.
sections.preview_head: Public diplomacy is one of the essential elements in the studies of politics and international relations today, not only from constructivist approaches, but also from more realistic and rationalist approaches (Tago, 2017). There is no consensus on this concept in the specialized literature, although a good definition would be from Sharp (2005: 106): 'the process by which direct relations with people in a country are pursued to advance the interests and extend the values of those being represented'. This definition encompasses the classic elements of public diplomacy: the government's communication with the foreign public, transmitting information and ideas, and values (Snow, 2009).

sections.paper_id: Moreno-Mercado and Calatrava-García - 2023 - Multilingual public diplomacy Strategic communication of Israeli Defence Forces (IDF) in Twitter du
paragraphs: This research aims to explore the Israeli army's institutional security and defence communication on Twitter. To this end, three official accounts have been analysed in different languages: @IDF (English), @FDIonline (Spanish) and @Tsahal_IDF (French). The main reason for focusing the study on Twitter is due to its flexibility to collect different volumes of data in different languages and moments, thanks to the several APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) available. However, official accounts in Hebrew (@idfonline), Russian (@IdfRussian) and Farsi (@IDFFarsi) are not the subject of this study for various reasons. First, because this research puts the spotlight on institutional security communication outside Israel's borders. And second, Russian and Iranian audiences are quantitatively inferior and subject to severe restrictions in social media (Alimardani and Michaelsen, 2021;Myles-Primakoff and Sherman, 2021). The sample (N) of the study is composed of 595 tweets. If we break down the data by language we get 199 tweets in English and 198 in both Spanish and French.

Potential audiences (based on the selected language variable) are found in societies with democratic political systems (The Economist, 2020; Freedom House, 2021) with high digitization and internet penetration (Digital Reports, 2021). Considering all the above and seeing the distribution of the sample, the following research questions arise: RQ1: Are there significant differences between the accounts? RQ2: Is the speech they use similar?

As a result of the above, it could be expected not to find relevant divergences in the three accounts analysed since the objective audience is framed in similar societies. That is, high degrees of freedom of expression and information and access to social networks. Therefore, the following hypotheses is formulated: H1: The Israeli army's Twitter accounts in English, French and Spanish use similar speeches when reporting on the conflict.

The case study chosen is the latest Israeli military operation in the Gaza Strip, called by the IDF 'Operation Guardian of the Walls' and 'Sword of Jerusalem' by Hamas and its affiliates. The study period runs from 6 May 2021 to 21 May 2021. Unrest in Jerusalem following incidents at the Temple Mount and Sheikh Jarrah sparked the biggest wave of violence since 2014. According to official IDF data, it launched approximately 3,150 rockets of which 90 percent were intercepted by the Iron Dome (IDF, 2021). During the 11 days of the military operation, significant events took place. To name a few, the destruction of a building that housed the headquarters of the Associated Press and Al Jazeera in Gaza; the death of Islamic Jihad commander Hasam Abu Harbid; or the serious clashes between Arabs and Jews in the cities of Lod and Ashkelon, among others.

The extracted data has been worked on in the Orange Data Mining software (Demšar et al., 2013). To study Twitter messages in the three selected accounts, the unsupervised LDA algorithm was used. This type of machine learning is one of the topic models most used by researchers in the social sciences (Hovy, 2020: 72). LDA models are very useful for identifying patterns and topics from the connection between various words. In other words, the task of the algorithm is simply to present a series of topics, at the choice of the researcher, that group words according to their frequency in them (Blei et al., 2003). This algorithm is a technique encompassed within Natural Language Processing (NLP) that is based on analysing the relationships between a set of documents and the terms they contain (so it is frequency-based) by producing concepts related to those documents and terms. The algorithm assumes that words that have similar meanings will co-occur in similar texts. The result is a set of topics present in each of the documents (a good explanation of its operation and usefulness can be found in Letsche and Berry, 1997, which shows that it is a fairly wellestablished technique). Currently, they are yielding very significant data in research on debates on Twitter, as in the case of speeches and psychological reactions to the problem of COVID-19 (Xue et al., 2020).

To deepen the study of the different accounts, an analysis of feelings has been carried out. Following García-Marín (2021: 34): 'Nowadays, there are different tools for measuring positive and negative texts, fundamentally sentiment analysis. This type of analysis, used routinely by both industry and academics, is related to the basis of many tools and computer algorithms.' Orange3 supports several possibilities for sentiment analysis including Liu Hu, Vader or SentiArt. However, when working with three different languages we have opted for the Multilingual function. This type of sentiment analysis is based on the use of a lexicon to catalogue the tone of a text from a keyword or bags of words. It is true that sentiment analysis has been widely criticized, but can be very useful if well-weighted dictionaries are used (Boukes et al., 2019). For this case we have used the lexicons available from Data Science Lab. However, there are other well-established lexicons such as ML-SentiCon for the case of Spanish (Cruz et al., 2014).
In short, the present study has followed these stages: (1) data collection;

(2) pre-processing the raw data (tokenization); (3) unsupervised machine learning (LDA); and (4) sentiment analysis. Our goal is to perform a simple but exhaustive analysis in a classic study that can be pigeonholed into so-called strategic studies; especially, because the contributions which are made by NPL techniques can be very useful for the demonstration of theories and hypotheses in a discipline that lacks quantitative analysis.

sections.paper_id: Moreno-Mercado and Calatrava-García - 2023 - Multilingual public diplomacy Strategic communication of Israeli Defence Forces (IDF) in Twitter du
paragraphs: The media dimension of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict has very specific dynamics and characteristics. This statement is easily contrasted in the use of certain concepts on aspects of daily reality in Palestine and Israel. Among other things we could point out the use of clearly antagonistic terms, such as 'security fence' or 'apartheid wall' to refer to the Israeli barrier of the West Bank (Rogers and Ben-David, 2010).

According to Truzman (2021), during Operation Guardian of the Walls more than 17 Palestinian factions fired rockets into Israeli territory. The Al-Qassam Brigades and Islamic Jihad were the most active organizations although there was guerrilla activity from other groups, such as the Jihad Jibril Brigades, Humat al-Aqsa or splits of the Martyrs of Al-Aqsa, among others. While it is true that a portion of Israeli citizens live in fear of terrorist attacks (Katz, 2021), much of the polarization of the conflict is due to the imbalance of power and strength (David vs Goliath). We must not forget that the treatment of Hamas as a terrorist group is a matter debated in multiple international and academic forums. In fact, the Palestinian organization operated a Twitter account (@ HamasInfoEn) between 2015 and 2018 before being cancelled. Following Margolin (2020), the group presented itself as a victim within the conflict and sought to legitimize itself as a legitimate voice of the Palestinian people. This debate on the treatment of Hamas has left very interesting facts. For example, the Spanish media clearly identify the group with the security problems in the region but there is not such a significant relationship between Hamas and the conflict (Moreno Mercado, 2021). In other words, threats to the region's security transcend Hamas' terrorist activities.

The first step of our analysis is to measure the frequency of tweets during the war. As can be seen in Figure 1, the increases in tweets are strongly related to specific events: (1) the launching of the first 150 rockets from the Gaza Strip (including the sound of sirens in Jerusalem), the blocking of routes to Gaza, the destruction of special targets and the death of Sergeant Omer Tabib (10-12 May); (2) during the 13-15 days, Hamas' Internal Security Headquarters was destroyed, 150 underground tunnels and more than 2,300 rockets were reported to have been fired; and (3) the latest increase in tweets is concentrated between 19 and 20 May, coinciding with the first news of a ceasefire and the destruction of 12 residences of senior Hamas commanders. In our view, these were expected results since the production of messages coincides with the most important military actions.

Table 1 presents the results with the most significant explanatory potential of our research. LDA allows topics to be grouped into various latent groups of words (from 1 to 12). For this reason, it is essential that researchers decide which number of topics yields more clarifying and relevant results for the research. After discarding LSI (Latent Semantic Indexing), since the texts are too homogeneous, it was decided to reduce our sample of tweets to three topics for each language. First, a more informative topic aimed at reporting on the attacks received from Gaza and the states of emergency in the different Israeli cities. Second, an issue that focuses on the military aspect of the conflict by highlighting the operations and objectives of the IDF. Furthermore, a third issue is aimed at assessing the situation with dissuasive messages. In other words, a more propagandistic issue of the hard power of the army. Although there are minor differences, we do not highlight significant divergences by language. One explanation could be that the potential audiences are similar (Western) and that many messages are translated (especially those containing audiovisual material). Therefore, we could redefine the themes in Defence, Operations, and Deterrence.

(1) 'IDF warplanes hit the main hub of operations of Hamas' internal security force in Gaza. The centre was a central part of Hamas' terrorist infrastructure. We provide advance notice and sufficient time to evacuate those inside. https://t.co/ vCe4P5Dv47' (@FDIonline, 17-05-2021). (2) ' Ours is to fight terrorism.

Hamas' aim is to attack Israeli civilians. In response to the incendiary balloons and the 4 rockets fired from Gaza into Israel yesterday, IDF tanks hit Hamas terrorist targets in Gaza'. (@FDIonline, 10-05-2021).

(3) 'The Iron Dome Aerial Defense System has one purpose -to intercept rockets mid-air before they can kill Israeli civilians. We will not apologize for saving lives. https://t.co/J0U8ct9bCt' (@IDF, 18-05-2021). (4) 'The Iron Dome Aerial Defense System just intercepted a Hamas UAV that crossed from Gaza into Israel. We will not tolerate any violation of Israeli sovereignty.' (@FDI, 12-05-2021). ( 5) '70% of our country is under fire. Will the UN Security Council treat Israel like the rest of the world? https://t.co/qBNRZMNAFm' (@Tsahal, 16-05-2021). ( 6) 'ALERT: 3 rockets were fired from Syria towards Israel, including 1 which fell on Syrian territory. https://t.co/iMskPfJjBW' (@Tsahal, 14-05-2021). Table 1. LDA analysis. sections.text:
The media dimension of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict has very specific dynamics and characteristics. This statement is easily contrasted in the use of certain concepts on aspects of daily reality in Palestine and Israel. Among other things we could point out the use of clearly antagonistic terms, such as 'security fence' or 'apartheid wall' to refer to the Israeli barrier of the West Bank (Rogers and Ben-David, 2010).

According to Truzman (2021), during Operation Guardian of the Walls more than 17 Palestinian factions fired rockets into Israeli territory. The Al-Qassam Brigades and Islamic Jihad were the most active organizations although there was guerrilla activity from other groups, such as the Jihad Jibril Brigades, Humat al-Aqsa or splits of the Martyrs of Al-Aqsa, among others. While it is true that a portion of Israeli citizens live in fear of terrorist attacks (Katz, 2021), much of the polarization of the conflict is due to the imbalance of power and strength (David vs Goliath). We must not forget that the treatment of Hamas as a terrorist group is a matter debated in multiple international and academic forums. In fact, the Palestinian organization operated a Twitter account (@ HamasInfoEn) between 2015 and 2018 before being cancelled. Following Margolin (2020), the group presented itself as a victim within the conflict and sought to legitimize itself as a legitimate voice of the Palestinian people. This debate on the treatment of Hamas has left very interesting facts. For example, the Spanish media clearly identify the group with the security problems in the region but there is not such a significant relationship between Hamas and the conflict (Moreno Mercado, 2021). In other words, threats to the region's security transcend Hamas' terrorist activities.

The first step of our analysis is to measure the frequency of tweets during the war. As can be seen in Figure 1, the increases in tweets are strongly related to specific events: (1) the launching of the first 150 rockets from the Gaza Strip (including the sound of sirens in Jerusalem), the blocking of routes to Gaza, the destruction of special targets and the death of Sergeant Omer Tabib (10-12 May); (2) during the 13-15 days, Hamas' Internal Security Headquarters was destroyed, 150 underground tunnels and more than 2,300 rockets were reported to have been fired; and (3) the latest increase in tweets is concentrated between 19 and 20 May, coinciding with the first news of a ceasefire and the destruction of 12 residences of senior Hamas commanders. In our view, these were expected results since the production of messages coincides with the most important military actions.

Table 1 presents the results with the most significant explanatory potential of our research. LDA allows topics to be grouped into various latent groups of words (from 1 to 12). For this reason, it is essential that researchers decide which number of topics yields more clarifying and relevant results for the research. After discarding LSI (Latent Semantic Indexing), since the texts are too homogeneous, it was decided to reduce our sample of tweets to three topics for each language. First, a more informative topic aimed at reporting on the attacks received from Gaza and the states of emergency in the different Israeli cities. Second, an issue that focuses on the military aspect of the conflict by highlighting the operations and objectives of the IDF. Furthermore, a third issue is aimed at assessing the situation with dissuasive messages. In other words, a more propagandistic issue of the hard power of the army. Although there are minor differences, we do not highlight significant divergences by language. One explanation could be that the potential audiences are similar (Western) and that many messages are translated (especially those containing audiovisual material). Therefore, we could redefine the themes in Defence, Operations, and Deterrence.

(1) 'IDF warplanes hit the main hub of operations of Hamas' internal security force in Gaza. The centre was a central part of Hamas' terrorist infrastructure. We provide advance notice and sufficient time to evacuate those inside. https://t.co/ vCe4P5Dv47' (@FDIonline, 17-05-2021). (2) ' Ours is to fight terrorism.

Hamas' aim is to attack Israeli civilians. In response to the incendiary balloons and the 4 rockets fired from Gaza into Israel yesterday, IDF tanks hit Hamas terrorist targets in Gaza'. (@FDIonline, 10-05-2021).

(3) 'The Iron Dome Aerial Defense System has one purpose -to intercept rockets mid-air before they can kill Israeli civilians. We will not apologize for saving lives. https://t.co/J0U8ct9bCt' (@IDF, 18-05-2021). (4) 'The Iron Dome Aerial Defense System just intercepted a Hamas UAV that crossed from Gaza into Israel. We will not tolerate any violation of Israeli sovereignty.' (@FDI, 12-05-2021). ( 5) '70% of our country is under fire. Will the UN Security Council treat Israel like the rest of the world? https://t.co/qBNRZMNAFm' (@Tsahal, 16-05-2021). ( 6) 'ALERT: 3 rockets were fired from Syria towards Israel, including 1 which fell on Syrian territory. https://t.co/iMskPfJjBW' (@Tsahal, 14-05-2021). Table 1. LDA analysis. sections.paper_id: Moreno-Mercado and Calatrava-García - 2023 - Multilingual public diplomacy Strategic communication of Israeli Defence Forces (IDF) in Twitter du
paragraphs: Through its Armed Forces, the Israeli government is developing an OPC campaign through social networks, specifically through its Twitter account. This campaign uses the news management of the Guardian of the Walls operation as a mechanism for promotion and information broadcasting. This campaign targets audiences in different countries, hence developing tweets in five languages (English, French, Spanish, Russian and Farsi). This target can be direct to the people or, mainly, indirectly through the media in these countries. The objective is to manage information within the international scene, and the goal is to increase the credibility of the Israeli government's actions. The results of this research show that accounts in English, French and Spanish have specific patterns. Therefore, we can establish that the hypothesis raised (H1) has been validated. As can be seen, both the number of messages created and the content of them can be framed within the same communication strategy. Image management is an essential issue in today's international relations and, in these respects, not all international actors are equal. Non-state actors may have an advantage, as states should logically be held much more accountable, and their actions should be more forcefully subject to international law. Within the Palestinian-Israeli conflict, the polarization of the different positions allows the different information to function as a sounding board. Thus, the Israeli government's public diplomacy actions should be considered part of a strategic communication framed within foreign policy objectives. In addition to this direct and immediate management of information, there are longer and more prolonged expressions of public diplomacy. There are proactive and strategic forms of communication to increase the favourable opinion of the State of Israel in its conflict with the Palestinians and Arab states in general (Simonsen, 2019;Yarchi, 2017).

The construction of the messages analysed has a double purpose: on the one hand, to provide excellent information in real-time about targets shot down, rockets intercepted and aggressions received, and as an easy and direct source for the traditional media. Moreover, amplifying Israeli views through potential influencers who sympathize with Israel and give greater visibility to the army's actions. In other words, relying on the legitimacy of other users with many more followers who have few ideological filters in the face of conflict.

Although we found few differences between the different language accounts, there is a prominent higher quantification of the English account. This was to be expected since the general public uses English. In addition, the importance of Israeli political developments in the US must be considered, especially during the Trump and Netanyahu administrations. For their part, both Spain and France have been two countries traditionally close to the Arab World and defenders of the rights of the Palestinian people. Thus, even in Latin America, there is an essential division of positions in this conflict, closely linked to ideological and ideological fractures.

As we have already indicated, OPC, which includes this strategic communication campaign carried out by the IDF, aims to transmit information intentionally. However, this need not mean manipulative information. Within the tweets studied in this article, it has become clear that the border between manipulative and non-manipulative forms of OPC (Bakir et al., 2018) has a very porous boundary. On the one hand, we have seen how objective information is transmitted (attacks, intercepted missiles, military operations). However, on the other hand, we could observe manipulative communication involving deception, incentivization, and even coercion. This last one, indirectly at least.

That makes sense because persuasion is 'a complex continuing interactive process, in which the communicator is trying to influence the communication partner to accept the proposed positions and behavioral patterns' (Vreg, 2000: 92). This assumption links persuasion directly to propaganda and to communication. So, each communicative persuasion contains elements of political propaganda. Therefore, there is a connotative manipulation and a false argument.

Another key issue would be the assessment of the success of these policies. This achievement is much more complicated to measure and is not the subject of this article. In any case, the number of retweets would indicate that the English account has a much greater reach than the Spanish and French accounts. What is certain is that the tools used in this article are research tools that facilitate hypotheses testing in conflict studies and international policy studies. The main problem is that it is often difficult to access all the data offered by social networks.

For their part, both Spain and France have been two countries traditionally close to the Arab World and defenders of the rights of the Palestinian people. Thus, even in Latin America, there is an essential division of positions in this conflict, closely linked to ideological and ideological fractures.

As we have already indicated, OPC, which includes this strategic communication campaign carried out by the IDF, aims to transmit information intentionally. However, this need not mean manipulative information. Within the tweets studied in this article, it has become clear that the border between manipulative and non-manipulative forms of OPC (Bakir et al., 2018) has a very porous boundary. On the one hand, we have seen how objective information is transmitted (attacks, intercepted missiles, military operations). However, on the other hand, we could observe manipulative communication involving deception, incentivization, and even coercion. This last one, indirectly at least.

That makes sense because persuasion is 'a complex continuing interactive process, in which the communicator is trying to influence the communication partner to accept the proposed positions and behavioral patterns' (Vreg, 2000: 92). This assumption links persuasion directly to propaganda and to communication. So, each communicative persuasion contains elements of political propaganda. Therefore, there is a connotative manipulation and a false argument.

Another key issue would be the assessment of the success of these policies. This achievement is much more complicated to measure and is not the subject of this article. In any case, the number of retweets would indicate that the English account has a much greater reach than the Spanish and French accounts. What is certain is that the tools used in this article are research tools that facilitate hypotheses testing in conflict studies and international policy studies. The main problem is that it is often difficult to access all the data offered by social networks.
sections.preview_head: Through its Armed Forces, the Israeli government is developing an OPC campaign through social networks, specifically through its Twitter account. This campaign uses the news management of the Guardian of the Walls operation as a mechanism for promotion and information broadcasting. This campaign targets audiences in different countries, hence developing tweets in five languages (English, French, Spanish, Russian and Farsi).
